featured.name: OpenJarvis
featured.tagline: Local-first framework for building on-device personal AI agents with tools, memory, and learning.
featured.why_radar: Stanford's take on local-first AI agents finally gives us a proper framework for building personal assistants that don't phone home. The focus on memory and learning makes this more than just another local LLM wrapper. We need more infrastructure like this to break the cloud dependency.

featured.name: Nemotron 3 Super
featured.tagline: 120B parameter open-source hybrid Mamba-Attention MoE model delivering 5x higher throughput for agentic AI.
featured.why_radar: NVIDIA's biggest open model yet bridges the gap between lightweight and frontier models with proper agentic focus. The 5x throughput claim and hybrid architecture could actually make 120B parameters usable in production. Finally, a model designed for agents rather than chat.

picks.name: Axe
picks.tagline: A 12MB binary that replaces your AI framework.
picks.why_radar: Sometimes the best tool is the smallest one. A 12MB replacement for bloated AI frameworks is exactly what edge computing needs. No dependencies, no Python overhead, just inference.

picks.name: OneCLI
picks.tagline: Vault for AI Agents in Rust.
picks.why_radar: Agent security is becoming critical as these systems handle more sensitive data. A Rust-based vault specifically for AI agents shows someone's thinking seriously about credential management. The timing is perfect as agent deployments scale.

picks.name: LogClaw
picks.tagline: Open-source AI SRE that auto-creates tickets from logs.
picks.why_radar: Finally, an AI SRE tool that doesn't require enterprise contracts. Auto-generating tickets from logs is exactly the kind of boring-but-essential automation we need more of. Open source means you can actually see how it makes decisions.

picks.name: MultiMind AI
picks.tagline: Run an Agent Council of LLMs that debate and synthesise answers.
picks.why_radar: Multi-agent debate systems are showing real promise for better reasoning. Having LLMs argue with each other before reaching consensus could be more reliable than single-model outputs. The council approach feels like a natural evolution of ensemble methods.

hn_top5.title: Launch HN: Spine Swarm (YC S23) – AI agents that collaborate on a visual canvas
hn_top5.summary: Spine Swarm promises AI agents that work together on visual canvases, which sounds impressive but feels like another attempt to reinvent collaborative software with AI buzzwords. The concept might have merit for specific workflows, but most teams probably don't need their AI agents to draw pretty pictures together.

hn_top5.title: Show HN: Context Gateway – Compress agent context before it hits the LLM
hn_top5.summary: Context Gateway tackles the real problem of bloated prompts eating up tokens and costs when using large language models. This is actually useful since most developers stuff way too much context into their prompts without thinking about efficiency.

hn_top5.title: Show HN: Simple plugin to get Claude Code to listen to you
hn_top5.summary: This plugin lets you talk to Claude instead of typing, which is handy for lazy developers who can't be bothered to use their keyboards. Voice coding sounds futuristic but usually ends up being slower and more error-prone than just typing properly.

hn_top5.title: Show HN: Hardened OpenClaw on AWS with Terraform
hn_top5.summary: Someone has hardened OpenClaw deployment on AWS using Terraform, which is practical infrastructure work that most people will ignore. It's the kind of unglamorous but necessary tooling that keeps systems running properly.

hn_top5.title: Show HN: HAL – Harmful Action Limiter: Lean command guard for AI coding agents.
hn_top5.summary: HAL tries to prevent AI coding agents from doing harmful things by acting as a command guard. With only 3 points and 9 comments, it seems the community isn't convinced this lightweight approach will stop determined AI misbehaviour.
